title: Primary Care and Global Health
section: Genetics, Aging, and Frontiers in Medicine

items.subtopic: Primary Care and Global Health
items.title: Understanding the Global Health Divide
items.content: The global health divide is characterized by significant disparities in life expectancy between high-income countries like Japan (84 years) and low-income countries such as Chad (54 years). This 30-year difference highlights the complex challenges faced by lower-income nations, including a mix of infectious and chronic diseases, and fundamental issues like insufficient food, water, sanitation, and education.
keyPoints: Life expectancy disparity: Japan (84 years) vs. Chad (54 years)
30-year life expectancy shortfall in Chad
Challenges include infectious/chronic diseases and lack of basic resources
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · Primary Care and Global Health

items.subtopic: Primary Care and Global Health
items.title: The Role of Primary Health Care in Bridging the Health Divide
items.content: The Declaration of Alma-Ata in 1978 emphasized health equity, aiming for 'health for all by the year 2000' through primary health care. Despite significant progress since then, achieving global health equity remains a challenge, especially after the COVID-19 pandemic.
keyPoints: Declaration of Alma-Ata (1978) aimed for 'health for all by the year 2000'
>45 years later, global health equity is still unachieved
COVID-19 pandemic has further highlighted ongoing challenges
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · Primary Care and Global Health

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.title: The Role of Primary Care in Health Systems
items.content: Primary care serves as the first point of contact for individuals seeking healthcare, addressing a majority of personal health needs and facilitating sustained patient-clinician partnerships within family and community contexts.
keyPoints: Primary care is defined by the U.S. Institute of Medicine (1996) as integrated, accessible services provided by accountable clinicians.
It functions as the initial contact point in healthcare systems, managing most health issues and referring patients when necessary.
Strong primary care is essential for the proper functioning of health systems across all income levels.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.title: Primary Health Care vs. Primary Care
items.content: While primary care focuses on direct patient services, primary health care encompasses a broader approach that includes addressing social and economic determinants of health and community involvement.
keyPoints: The Declaration of Alma-Ata (1978) emphasized the need for cross-sector collaboration to address social and economic factors impacting health.
Primary health care aims to bring services closer to communities, ensuring their participation in healthcare decisions.
It addresses essential aspects like water, sanitation, and education that are crucial for overall health.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.title: Challenges in Achieving 'Health for All'
items.content: Despite the goals set by the Declaration of Alma-Ata, many countries failed to achieve 'health for all' due to incomplete implementation of primary health care principles and shifting global priorities.
keyPoints: The target of 'health for all by the year 2000' was not met, largely due to insufficient cross-sector work and resource allocation.
Economic challenges led to a shift in focus from comprehensive primary health care to targeted public health programs like immunization and child survival initiatives.
By the 1990s, primary health care lost favor as low- and middle-income countries were encouraged to prioritize cost-effectiveness over broad-based healthcare reforms.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.title: Life Expectancy Disparities Between High- and Low-Income Countries
items.content: Low- and middle-income countries face significant health challenges with life expectancy at birth for girls being 65 years, compared to 83 years in high-income countries. This gap is attributed not just to poverty but also to social organizational failures.
keyPoints: Defined by a per-capita gross national income of <$12,535 (U.S.) per person per year
>85% of the world's population resides in these regions
Life expectancy for girls: 65 years in low-income countries vs. 83 years in high-income countries
Health disparities persist despite global progress in life expectancy over past 40 years
"People are sick not primarily because they are poor but due to other social organizational failures" - Angus Deaton
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.title: Impact of Social and Economic Factors on Health Disparities
items.content: Health disparities in low- and middle-income countries are largely driven by social and economic factors, including daily living conditions and access to resources. The health care sector often exacerbates these inequalities.
keyPoints: Disparities result from social and economic factors like living conditions and resource access
Inverse-care law: poor communities less likely to benefit from public health services
Reducing childhood mortality in low- and middle-income countries to that of the richest fifth could decrease global rates by 40%
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.title: Barriers to Effective Health Care Delivery in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
items.content: Despite the availability of scientifically validated, low-cost health interventions, their implementation is hindered by systemic issues within health systems, social stratification, and scientific biases.
keyPoints: Proven interventions exist but are underutilized (e.g., insecticide-treated bed nets for malaria)
Three identified barriers: shortfalls in health system performance, stratifying social conditions, skews in science
Health progress is slow due to issues with the 'how' of healthcare delivery
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS
items.title: Why Health Financing in Low- and Middle-Income Countries is Insufficient
items.content: In low- and middle-income countries, health financing is critically inadequate compared to high-income nations. Public spending on health averages less than 3% of GDP for middle-income countries and less than 2% for low-income ones, while external funding remains a minor fraction of total expenditures.
keyPoints: High-income countries spend >7% of GDP on health
Middle-income countries spend <3% of GDP on health
Low-income countries spend <2% of GDP on health
External financing for health is $37 billion, representing <2% of total expenditures in low- and middle-income countries
70% of health expenditures in Africa come from domestic sources
Point-of-service charges are inefficient and inequitable
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS
items.title: Impact of Health Worker Shortage on Global Disease Burden
items.content: A significant shortage of health workers, estimated at over 18 million globally, severely impacts healthcare delivery in low- and middle-income countries. Sub-Saharan Africa bears a disproportionate disease burden with only 3% of the global health workforce.
keyPoints: >18 million global shortage of health workers
Sub-Saharan Africa has 24% of the global disease burden but only 3% of the health workforce
Migration exacerbates health worker shortages in low- and middle-income countries
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.title: Impact of Social Inequality on Health Care Access
items.content: Social and economic forces create barriers to equitable health care delivery, particularly affecting marginalized populations such as the urban poor and illiterate mothers. These groups face significant challenges in accessing necessary medical services due to financial constraints, inadequate infrastructure, and systemic discrimination.
keyPoints: Health care systems are influenced by social and economic stratification.
Marginalized populations have disproportionately large health needs.
Examples include urban poor unable to afford transportation for clinic visits and rural mothers struggling to access care for infants with febrile convulsions.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.title: Research and Development Imbalance in Global Health
items.content: There is a significant imbalance in research and development investments, with low- and middle-income countries receiving inadequate resources for addressing their specific health needs. Efforts are being made to address this through initiatives like the TB Alliance's development of new tuberculosis drugs.
keyPoints: Low- and middle-income countries face major health problems due to insufficient R&D investment.
TB Alliance developed pretomanid, approved in 2019 as part of a regimen (BPaL) for treating extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis.
Global vaccine inequity during the COVID-19 pandemic highlights ongoing research and development imbalances.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.title: Challenges in Health Care Delivery Systems
items.content: The primary constraint on health improvement in low- and middle-income countries is not the lack of health technologies but their effective delivery. There is a bias towards funding new technologies over research into improving health care delivery systems, which requires significant investment in scientific rigor.
keyPoints: Effective delivery of health technologies is a major challenge.
Most health research financing focuses on developing new drugs, vaccines, and diagnostics.
There is a need for investment in understanding how to improve health care delivery systems amidst adverse social conditions.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY
items.title: Why Strengthening Primary Health Care is Crucial in Addressing Global Health Challenges
items.content: Strengthening primary health care (PHC) is essential to address global health challenges such as disparities in health, rising healthcare costs, and dissatisfaction with accessible care. Effective PHC can tackle noncommunicable diseases, pandemics, and climate change-related health threats.
keyPoints: Primary health care resurgence driven by issues like increasing health disparities and spiraling healthcare costs.
PHC is vital for addressing noncommunicable diseases, pandemics, and climate change impacts on health.
Strong PHC systems are needed to sustain public health gains in areas such as HIV/AIDS and pandemic preparedness.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY
items.title: How Weak Health Systems Hinder Access to New Technologies
items.content: Weak health systems are a significant barrier to delivering new medical technologies, such as COVID-19 vaccines and antiretroviral therapy, to those in need. Strengthening these systems is crucial for equitable access.
keyPoints: Weak health systems obstruct the delivery of essential new technologies like COVID-19 vaccines.
Addressing social and economic factors is necessary alongside robust health systems for effective disease management.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Components of Primary Health Care Reframing
items.content: The 2019 World Health Assembly resolution emphasized the need for primary health care to be based on three core components: integrated health services with primary care and essential public health functions, empowerment of people and communities, and multisectoral policy and action.
keyPoints: Primary health care refocused as: (1) integrated health services; (2) empowered people and communities; (3) multisectoral policy and action.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Universal Coverage Reforms for Health Equity
items.content: To address health disparities, universal coverage reforms aim to ensure access to comprehensive services without financial ruin. This involves prepayment financing systems and eliminating user fees at the point of service.
keyPoints: More than 100 million people driven into extreme poverty annually by healthcare costs.
Universal coverage requires: availability of services to all, elimination of access barriers, pooled financing mechanisms (e.g., taxation or insurance).
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.title: Service Delivery Reforms for People-Centered Health Systems
items.content: Reforming service delivery to focus on people's needs involves decentralizing services and developing primary care that addresses both noncommunicable and communicable diseases.
keyPoints: Health systems often organized around provider needs, leading to centralized or vertical programs.
People-centered approach aims for effective and appropriate health care.
Need for reform in low- and middle-income countries due to rising noncommunicable disease burden.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.title: The Importance of Continuity in Primary Care
items.content: Continuity and comprehensiveness in primary care are crucial for effective patient management, ensuring ongoing personal relationships with a care team to navigate the health system efficiently.
keyPoints: Primary care must address all presenting problems in a unified way.
Enhanced understanding of patients' social contexts improves continuity of care.
Ongoing personal relationship with a care team ensures comprehensive and continuous care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.title: Role of Public Policy in Health Equity
items.content: Public policies across various sectors are essential to reduce health disparities and achieve global public health targets, as demonstrated by successful multisectoral actions during the COVID-19 pandemic.
keyPoints: Multisectoral policies address health inequities at local, national, and global levels.
Successful management of pandemics requires coordination across societies for nonpharmaceutical measures.
Tobacco control exemplifies potential improvements through multisectoral collaboration.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.title: Leadership Reforms in Health Governance
items.content: Effective health governance requires leadership reforms that engage diverse actors, including NGOs and the private sector, to address contemporary health challenges.
keyPoints: Participation is crucial at all levels of decision-making in healthcare.
Governments must guide and regulate among various organizations involved in healthcare delivery.
Global health governance mechanisms are needed due to the transnational nature of health issues.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Impact of National Scale Primary Care Initiatives
items.content: Investment in primary care on a national scale, as seen in China post-WWII, can significantly enhance life expectancy and health coverage.
keyPoints: China's investment in infrastructure for public health (e.g., water and sanitation) linked with community health workers ('barefoot doctors') doubled life expectancy within 20 years.
Barefoot doctors provided basic medical interventions including immunizations, maternal care, and antibiotics.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Successful Primary Care Models in Low-Income Settings
items.content: Countries like Costa Rica, Sri Lanka, Kerala, and Cuba achieved good health care at low cost through primary care services.
keyPoints: Despite lower economic development and health spending, these countries had health indicators approaching or exceeding those of developed nations.
Common strategies included free or low-cost care expansion to entire populations with community participation in decision-making.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Brazil's Family Health Program and its Impact
items.content: The Brazilian Family Health Program expanded primary care access, improving population health outcomes.
keyPoints: From 1980 to 2010, Brazil achieved universal coverage through the Family Health Program.
Program teams included diverse healthcare professionals responsible for all individuals in a geographic area.
Evidence shows significant gains in childhood mortality and reduced health inequities.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.title: Impact of Primary Care on Health Outcomes in Bangladesh
items.content: Bangladesh's dramatic increase in life expectancy and reduction in childhood mortality rates highlight the critical role of primary care expansion, driven by NGOs focusing on microcredit, education, and healthcare.
keyPoints: Life expectancy increased significantly since 1971.
Childhood mortality rates are lower than those in India and Pakistan due to expanded access to primary care services.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.title: Consequences of Undermining Primary Care: Sub-Saharan Africa
items.content: In Sub-Saharan Africa, the erosion of primary care services led to severe health outcome reversals during the HIV/AIDS epidemic. Zimbabwe's political turmoil exacerbated these issues.
keyPoints: Primary care service undermining contributed to catastrophic health outcomes in the HIV/AIDS era.
Zimbabwe experienced a decline in health and social services due to political instability.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW-AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.title: Health Policy Changes and Their Impact: The Case of China
items.content: China's health system unraveling in the early 1980s due to privatization led to increased out-of-pocket expenses, reduced access to primary care, and stagnation in health progress despite economic growth.
keyPoints: Privatization in the 1980s led to a breakdown of universal health coverage.
By the end of the 1980s, most Chinese paid directly for healthcare with little insurance coverage.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)
items.title: Strengthening Primary Health Care in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
items.content: China and India have implemented measures to enhance primary health care by increasing government funding, restoring health insurance, and setting targets for universal access. These efforts are part of a broader trend linked to the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
keyPoints: China increased government funding and enacted a target of universal access to primary care services.
India expanded primary care services in rural and urban areas using mobilized funding.
Both countries use public resources from growing economies to fund primary care.
Nearly all countries have adopted universal health coverage over the past decade.
Primary health care is key to achieving affordable, timely quality health services.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)
items.title: Linking Primary Health Care to Sustainable Development Goals
items.content: The efforts by China and India to strengthen primary health care are aligned with the UN's goal of universal health coverage, emphasizing timely and affordable quality health services.
keyPoints: Primary health care initiatives in China and India support UN Sustainable Development Goal number 3.
Universal health coverage involves providing quality health services affordably and timely.
Adoption of universal health coverage has been nearly universal over the past decade.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Importance of Global Health Initiatives in Strengthening Primary Care
items.content: Global health initiatives can significantly enhance primary care by working synergistically with national health systems, avoiding undermining existing resources. These initiatives are crucial for addressing systemic challenges and improving access to comprehensive primary care services.
keyPoints: Global health spending reached $9 trillion in 2020, more than double from a decade earlier.
GHIs like the Global Fund and COVAX have shown potential but also face challenges in equitable vaccine distribution.
Coordination of funding is essential to reduce fragmentation and strengthen national health systems.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Role of Community Health Workers in Expanding Primary Care
items.content: Community health workers can effectively extend primary care services, especially in low-resource settings. They provide essential health interventions and improve outcomes by being part of multidisciplinary teams supported by physicians.
keyPoints: In Ethiopia, over 38,000 community health workers have been deployed to enhance primary care access.
India has recruited more than 600,000 community health advocates for rural primary care services.
Community health worker programs in Niger have reduced childhood mortality and health disparities.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.title: Enhancing Primary Care through Social Participation and Innovation
items.content: Increased social participation and innovative approaches can drive the development of primary care services. Community involvement in health decision-making ensures that services meet broader needs rather than just specific public health priorities.
keyPoints: Political pressure from community advocates has accelerated progress in primary care without major funding increases.
Entrepreneurial initiatives by NGOs have scaled up community-based services effectively.
Population participation in healthcare provision and decision-making leads to more holistic care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Leveraging Technology for Primary Care Expansion in Low- and Middle-Income Countries
items.content: Recent advancements in information and communication technologies, such as mobile phones and the Internet, offer significant opportunities to enhance primary care services in low- and middle-income countries. These technologies can facilitate e-health and telemedicine initiatives, potentially allowing these health systems to surpass those of wealthier nations by bypassing outdated legacy systems.
keyPoints: Potential for systematic implementation of e-health and telemedicine
Leapfrogging opportunity due to less encumbered legacy systems
Rapid rollout of mobile networks during COVID-19 increased digital health uptake
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Global Health Equity and the Role of Primary Care
items.content: The commitment to addressing global health inequities is underscored by initiatives like the United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals, which aim for universal health coverage by 2030. Primary care serves as a crucial framework for translating shared values of health equity across different contexts.
keyPoints: United Nations' Sustainable Development Goals emphasize equity
Target: Universal health coverage by 2030
Primary care as a framework for effective health equity translation
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.title: Reform Strategies for Enhancing Global Health Systems
items.content: To address health inequities and improve global health systems, four key reform strategies are essential: organizing healthcare around community needs, integrating non-health sectors to promote health, establishing equitable financing mechanisms, and investing in leadership. These reforms aim to create a sustainable primary care system that can effectively respond to shared global health challenges.
keyPoints: Four types of reform for health systems
Organizing services around people's needs
Harnessing non-health sectors for better health outcomes
Equitable financing for universal coverage
Investment in leadership for change
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: Primary Care and Global Health
items.question: Which document crystallized a vision of justice in health regardless of income, gender, ethnicity, or education?
options: The Declaration of Geneva
The Alma-Ata Declaration
The Ottawa Charter for Health Promotion
The Millennium Development Goals
items.explanation: The Declaration of Alma-Ata in 1978 called for 'health for all by the year 2000' through primary health care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · Primary Care and Global Health

items.subtopic: Primary Care and Global Health
items.question: What is one fundamental challenge faced by low- and middle-income countries in improving health?
options: Lack of technological advancements
Excessive healthcare funding
Fragility of foundational health determinants like food, water, sanitation, and education
Overabundance of healthcare professionals
items.explanation: Low- and middle-income countries face challenges with the fragility of foundations underlying good health such as sufficient food, water, sanitation, and education.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · Primary Care and Global Health

items.subtopic: Primary Care and Global Health
items.question: What was one of the goals outlined in the Declaration of Alma-Ata?
options: Achieving universal secondary care
Promoting private healthcare systems
Health for all by the year 2000 through primary health care
Eliminating infectious diseases globally
items.explanation: The Declaration of Alma-Ata called for 'health for all by the year 2000' through primary health care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · Primary Care and Global Health

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.question: According to the U.S. Institute of Medicine's definition from 1996, which of the following is NOT a characteristic of primary care?
options: The provision of integrated, accessible health care services by clinicians.
Clinicians are accountable for addressing a large majority of personal health care needs.
Developing a sustained partnership with patients.
Focusing exclusively on hospital-based systems.
items.explanation: Primary care involves integrated, accessible services and partnerships, not exclusive focus on hospitals.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.question: What was a key goal of the Declaration of Alma-Ata?
options: To establish high-technology services in urban areas.
To achieve 'health for all by the year 2000'.
To reduce public sector spending on health.
To concentrate solely on immunization programs.
items.explanation: The Declaration of Alma-Ata aimed to achieve 'health for all by the year 2000'.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.question: Which aspect was unexpectedly seen in high-income countries after implementing primary health care reforms?
options: A decline in the number of primary care physicians.
Progress in strengthening primary care services.
An increase in hospital-based systems similar to low-income countries.
A shift away from family medicine as a specialty.
items.explanation: High-income countries saw progress in strengthening primary care services after reforms.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.question: What contributed to the missed target of 'health for all by the year 2000'?
options: Successful implementation of all aspects of primary health care.
Failure to address social and economic factors affecting health.
Increased global commitment to comprehensive primary health care.
Universal access to high-technology services in urban areas.
items.explanation: The target was missed partly due to failure to implement all aspects of the primary health care approach, including addressing social and economic factors.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.question: What is the primary reason for health disparities in low- and middle-income countries according to Angus Deaton?
options: People are sick primarily because they are poor.
Health delivery failures, not just poverty, contribute significantly to sickness.
Economic growth automatically improves health outcomes.
High fertility rates are the main cause of health issues.
items.explanation: Angus Deaton stated that people in poor countries are sick due to social organizational failures like health delivery, not just poverty.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME CATEGORIES
items.question: Which of the following interventions is NOT mentioned as a scientifically validated low-cost intervention for improving health in low- and middle-income countries?
options: Insecticide-treated bed nets for malaria.
Taxes on cigarettes to reduce smoking rates.
Short-course chemotherapy for tuberculosis.
Vaccination programs for all preventable diseases.
items.explanation: The passage mentions short-course chemotherapy for tuberculosis, but not vaccination programs as a low-cost intervention discussed.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.question: What is the life expectancy at birth for a girl in high-income countries compared to that in low-income countries?
options: 83 years and 65 years, respectively.
70 years and 60 years, respectively.
75 years and 55 years, respectively.
80 years and 50 years, respectively.
items.explanation: The passage states that the life expectancy at birth for a girl in high-income countries is 83 years, compared to 65 years in low-income countries.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.question: Which of these health challenges has emerged rapidly due to urbanization and lifestyle changes in low- and middle-income countries?
options: Infectious diseases.
Chronic diseases, environmental overcrowding, and road traffic injuries.
High fertility rates.
Maternal mortality.
items.explanation: The passage notes that chronic diseases, environmental overcrowding, and road traffic injuries have emerged rapidly due to urbanization and lifestyle changes.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS
items.question: What percentage of the gross domestic product do high-income countries spend on health, on average?
items.explanation: High-income countries spend, on average, more than 7% of their GDP on health.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS
items.question: What is the predominant form of healthcare financing in low- and middle-income countries?
options: External donor funding
Government subsidies
Charging patients at the point of service
Insurance premiums
items.explanation: The predominant form of health care financing in these regions is charging patients at the point of service.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS
items.question: What percentage of global disease burden does Sub-Saharan Africa carry?
items.explanation: Sub-Saharan Africa carries 24% of the global disease burden.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · SHORTFALLS IN PERFORMANCE OF HEALTH SYSTEMS

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.question: Which of the following is NOT mentioned as a force that affects equitable healthcare delivery?
options: Shaky or nonexistent social security systems
Dangerous work environments
High-income countries' research investments
Systematic discrimination against racialized minorities
items.explanation: High-income countries' research investments are not mentioned as a force affecting equitable healthcare delivery.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.question: What is one of the primary constraints on better health in low- and middle-income countries?
options: Availability of health technologies
Effective delivery of health technologies
Lack of scientific rigor
Insufficient research funding
items.explanation: The primary constraint is related less to the availability of health technologies and more to their effective delivery.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.question: Which organization revitalized a previously 'dry' pipeline for new tuberculosis drugs?
options: World Health Organization (WHO)
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
TB Alliance
Global Fund
items.explanation: The TB Alliance revitalized a previously 'dry' pipeline for new tuberculosis drugs.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.question: What is one of the Sustainable Development Goal targets adopted in Agenda 2030?
options: Universal primary education
Gender equality
Universal health coverage
Climate action
items.explanation: Universal health coverage is enshrined as one of the Sustainable Development Goal targets adopted in Agenda 2030.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY
items.question: What has driven the resurgence of interest in primary health care in the twenty-first century?
options: Rapidly increasing disparities in health between and within countries, spiraling costs of healthcare, dissatisfaction with accessible care, and failure to address noncommunicable diseases.
The development of new medical technologies like COVID-19 vaccines.
Increased funding from global health development agencies.
A decline in the incidence of communicable diseases.
items.explanation: These factors mirror those that led to the Declaration of Alma-Ata, highlighting persistent and emerging challenges.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY
items.question: Why are strong health systems with effective primary care necessary in addressing global health challenges?
options: To ensure the delivery of new technologies such as COVID-19 vaccines and antiretroviral therapy.
To reduce the need for specialized medical training.
To focus solely on noncommunicable diseases.
To decrease healthcare costs by eliminating primary care.
items.explanation: Strong health systems are crucial to overcoming obstacles in delivering essential technologies and addressing comprehensive health threats.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY

items.subtopic: PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY
items.question: What is a major obstacle to delivering new medical technologies like COVID-19 vaccines?
options: The rapid development of these technologies.
Weak health systems.
Overabundance of healthcare resources.
Excessive funding from global agencies.
items.explanation: Inadequate health infrastructure hinders the distribution and access to new medical advancements.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY HEALTH CARE IN THE TWENTY-FIRST CENTURY

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: What were the three components of primary health care as reframed by the 2019 World Health Assembly?
options: (1) Primary care and essential public health functions; (2) Empowered people and communities; (3) Multisectoral policy and action
(1) Universal coverage reforms; (2) Service delivery reforms; (3) Financial sustainability
(1) Centralized services; (2) Vertical programs; (3) Disease-specific interventions
(1) Primary care services; (2) Health insurance systems; (3) Community health workers
items.explanation: The resolution reframed primary health care into three components: primary care and essential public health functions, empowered people and communities, and multisectoral policy and action.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: What is a major priority in low- and middle-income countries to address disparities caused by healthcare costs?
options: Implementing vertical programs for single diseases
Expanding health services in urban areas
Moving toward prepayment financing systems for universal coverage
Centralizing health care services around clinicians
items.explanation: A major priority is moving toward prepayment financing systems for universal coverage to ensure access without causing economic ruin.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: Which approach aims to reorient care around the needs of people in health systems?
options: Vertical programs targeting single diseases
Centralized service delivery
People-centered approach
Financial sustainability reforms
items.explanation: The people-centered approach aims to reorient care around the needs of the people served by health systems.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.question: What is required for moving toward universal health coverage according to the passage?
options: Providing services only in urban areas
Ensuring availability of health care services, eliminating access barriers, and organizing pooled financing mechanisms
Focusing solely on communicable diseases
Centralizing all health services
items.explanation: Universal coverage requires ensuring service availability to all, eliminating access barriers, and organizing pooled financing mechanisms.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: What is essential to ensure comprehensiveness and continuity of care in primary health care?
options: People having an ongoing personal relationship with a care team
Frequent changes in healthcare providers for patients
Limited interaction between patients and clinicians
Discontinuity of care post-hospital discharge
items.explanation: Comprehensiveness and continuity are best achieved by ensuring ongoing personal relationships with a care team.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: What role does primary care play in the health system according to the passage?
options: Providing only curative interventions
Navigating people through the health system and monitoring referrals
Focusing solely on health promotion activities
Handling all medical issues without any referral
items.explanation: Primary care navigates people through the health system and monitors consultations when referred elsewhere.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: Why is it important for primary care providers to understand patients' social context?
options: To enhance communication and address presenting problems comprehensively
To limit the time spent with each patient
To focus only on medical treatments without considering other factors
To ensure that patients are referred frequently
items.explanation: Understanding social context enhances communication and allows for comprehensive addressing of presenting problems.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.question: What is a common issue faced by people after being discharged from hospitals?
options: They receive continuous care without any issues
They often lose contact with the health system and do not benefit from interventions
They are immediately integrated into primary care services
They have no need for follow-up consultations
items.explanation: People often lose contact with the healthcare system after discharge, missing out on benefits of complex interventions.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: Which country's primary care model involved 'barefoot doctors' who provided basic medical interventions in rural areas?
options: China
Costa Rica
Sri Lanka
Kerala
items.explanation: China implemented the 'barefoot doctor' program to provide primary care services in rural villages.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: What was a common feature among China, Costa Rica, Sri Lanka, Kerala, and Cuba that contributed to their successful health outcomes?
options: High levels of economic development
Expansion of care free or at low cost with community participation
Exclusive focus on urban healthcare infrastructure
Privatization of primary care services
items.explanation: These countries emphasized primary care services, expanded them to the entire population for free or at low cost, and involved community participation.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: What major healthcare program did Brazil implement from 1980 to 2010 to increase the availability of health care?
options: Brazilian Universal Health Care
Family Health Program
National Primary Care Initiative
Community Health Worker Expansion
items.explanation: The Brazilian Family Health Program was implemented to expand primary care services across the country.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: Which measure has Chile instituted to specify patient rights in terms of service entitlement?
options: National Health Assembly
Community Health Charter
Patient's Charter
Social Welfare Benefits
items.explanation: Chile instituted a Patient's Charter that specifies the range of services to which patients are entitled.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.question: Which country's primary health care progress was significantly influenced by a vibrant NGO community focusing on poor women and their families?
options: Bangladesh
Thailand
Botswana
China
items.explanation: Bangladesh has seen progress in primary health care due to the efforts of a vibrant NGO community focusing on poor women and their families.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.question: What was one major consequence of China's decision to open up its economy in the early 1980s?
options: Expansion of universal health coverage
Rapid privatization of the health sector
Stabilization of primary care services
Increase in NGO involvement
items.explanation: China's decision to open up its economy led to rapid privatization of the health sector and breakdown of universal health coverage.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.question: Which country has managed to maintain primary care services better than Zimbabwe during the HIV/AIDS epidemic?
options: Botswana
Ethiopia
Vietnam
Oman
items.explanation: Botswana maintained primary care services more effectively than Zimbabwe during the HIV/AIDS epidemic.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.question: What is a common negative feature of health systems in China and India due to weak primary care?
options: Focus on preventive care
Disproportionate focus on specialty services
Universal health coverage
Regulated commercialization
items.explanation: Both countries have a disproportionate focus on specialty services provided in hospitals due to weak primary care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)
items.question: Which of the following measures has China implemented to strengthen primary health care?
options: Increased government funding of health care
Reduced government involvement in health insurance
Implemented a target for universal access to tertiary care services
Decreased public resources allocated from its growing economy
items.explanation: China has increased government funding of health care and enacted a target of universal access to primary care services.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)
items.question: What is one way India has expanded primary care services?
options: Reduced funding for rural health services
Mobilized funding to expand primary care in urban settings only
Greatly expanded primary care services in both rural and urban areas
Focused solely on tertiary care expansion
items.explanation: India has mobilized funding to greatly expand primary care services in both rural areas and urban settings.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)
items.question: What is linked to goal number 3 of the UN Sustainable Development Goals according to the passage?
options: The provision of quality health services in a timely manner at affordable cost
Reduction of primary care services
Decrease in government funding for healthcare
Focus on tertiary care over primary care
items.explanation: Universal health coverage, which includes the provision of quality health services in a timely manner at affordable cost, is linked to goal number 3 of the UN Sustainable Development Goals.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: What is one of the main reasons for the increased spending on health in low- and middle-income countries?
options: A greater emphasis on building health systems based on primary care.
An increase in the prevalence of infectious diseases.
The reduction in global health initiatives funding.
Decreased government involvement in healthcare.
items.explanation: Increased spending is partly due to a greater emphasis on building health systems based on primary care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: Which global initiative failed to ensure equitable access to COVID-19 vaccines?
options: The Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria.
The U.S. President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief.
The COVAX facility.
The Wellcome Trust Future of GHIs.
items.explanation: The COVAX facility failed to ensure equitable access to COVID-19 vaccines.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: What is a key benefit of community health workers in low-income countries?
options: They can replace physicians entirely.
They require extensive training and high salaries.
They improve access to primary care services.
They focus solely on infectious disease management.
items.explanation: Community health workers have been shown to improve access to primary care services.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.question: What is a systemic concern related to global health initiatives in low-income countries?
options: They always undermine staffing and procurement of supplies.
They fail to improve access to targeted health services.
They may not increase domestic resources for comprehensive primary care.
They eliminate the need for national health systems.
items.explanation: A systemic concern is that GHIs may not effectively increase domestic resources for comprehensive primary care.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the four types of reform mentioned for promoting health equity?
options: Organizing health care services around the needs of people and communities.
Harnessing services and sectors beyond health care to promote and protect health more effectively.
Establishing sustainable and equitable financing mechanisms for universal health coverage.
Implementing widespread vaccination programs.
items.explanation: The passage does not mention implementing widespread vaccination programs as one of the four types of reform.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: What is a potential advantage for health systems in low- and middle-income countries according to the passage?
options: They have more financial resources than high-income countries.
They are less encumbered by legacy systems that are hard to modernize.
They already have universal health coverage.
They have better infrastructure for health services.
items.explanation: The passage states these countries could leapfrog wealthier counterparts due to being less encumbered by legacy systems.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: What has been demonstrated during the COVID-19 pandemic regarding health services in low-income countries?
options: Decreased use of telemedicine.
Increased uptake of telemedicine and digital clinical support.
Complete abandonment of traditional healthcare systems.
Universal implementation of e-health initiatives.
items.explanation: The passage mentions a greatly increased uptake of the use of telemedicine during the COVID-19 pandemic.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.question: What is one of the Sustainable Development Goals related to health?
options: Achieving universal primary education by 2030.
Reducing global carbon emissions by half by 2030.
Achievement of universal health coverage in all countries by 2030.
Eliminating poverty worldwide by 2025.
items.explanation: The passage states there is a specific target on the achievement of universal health coverage in all countries by 2030.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.statement: Community-based services in low-income countries have only been scaled up through major increases in funding.
items.explanation: Progress has been accelerated by community advocates and entrepreneurial initiatives without major increases in funding.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.statement: The COVID-19 pandemic has highlighted the inequities in global health systems.
items.explanation: The passage states that shortfalls in health system performance and social conditions leading to unfair differences have been more visible during the pandemic.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.statement: The primary health care approach is considered ineffective in promoting global health equity.
items.explanation: The passage describes the primary health care system as one of the most effective systems for promoting health equity.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: Primary Care and Global Health
items.assertion: Achieving global health equity remains a challenge.
items.reason: More than 45 years after the Declaration of Alma-Ata, significant disparities in health persist globally.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true; the reason correctly explains why achieving global health equity is still challenging.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · Primary Care and Global Health

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.assertion: Primary care is essential for the proper functioning of health systems.
items.reason: It provides the first point of contact when people seek health care and deals with most problems.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains why primary care is essential.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE
items.assertion: The global health community maintained a strong commitment to primary health care after Alma-Ata.
items.reason: Economic recession tempered enthusiasm for comprehensive primary health care approaches.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason is true, as economic factors led to reduced commitment.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · PRIMARY CARE AND PRIMARY HEALTH CARE

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.assertion: Health status in low- and middle-income countries is uniform across all nations.
items.reason: Nations such as Cuba and Costa Rica have life expectancies similar to high-income countries, while others like those in Sub-Saharan Africa do not.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; health status varies significantly among low- and middle-income countries.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES
items.assertion: Health care systems in low- and middle-income countries exacerbate health inequalities.
items.reason: The inverse-care law states that poor communities are less likely to benefit from public health services than better-off ones.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the reason correctly explaining the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · HEALTH CHALLENGES IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.assertion: Efforts for more equitable health care delivery must contend with shaky or nonexistent social security systems.
items.reason: Shaky or nonexistent social security systems are among the forces affecting equitable healthcare delivery.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE
items.assertion: The primary constraint on better health in low- and middle-income countries is the lack of new health technologies.
items.reason: Health research financing focuses more on delivery systems than on developing new technologies.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the primary constraint is related to effective delivery, not lack of new technologies.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · STRATIFYING SOCIAL CONDITIONS | SKEWS IN SCIENCE

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.assertion: Universal coverage reforms are essential for reducing health disparities.
items.reason: These reforms ensure access to comprehensive services without causing economic ruin.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, with the latter correctly explaining why universal coverage reforms reduce disparities.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)
items.assertion: The increase in noncommunicable diseases necessitates urgent reform of service delivery.
items.reason: Many low- and middle-income countries have already adapted their health systems to address these diseases effectively.
items.explanation: While the assertion is true, the reason is false as many countries are only starting to adapt their health systems.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 1)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.assertion: Leadership reforms are necessary to make health authorities more reliable.
items.reason: Effective leadership acknowledges the role of government and diverse organizations in healthcare delivery.
items.explanation: Both assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the necessity for leadership reforms.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)
items.assertion: The Declaration of Alma-Ata emphasized participation only at the patient level.
items.reason: Participation is important at all levels of decision-making in health care.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; participation is emphasized at all levels, not just the patient level.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · REVITALIZATION OF PRIMARY HEALTH CARE (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.assertion: China's primary care expansion included massive investments in infrastructure for public health.
items.reason: These investments were linked to the innovative use of community health workers known as 'barefoot doctors'.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.assertion: The Brazilian Family Health Program only provided care to those who accessed health clinics.
items.reason: Primary care teams were responsible for providing care to all people in a specified geographic area, not just clinic visitors.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the program aimed to provide care to everyone in a designated area, regardless of clinic visits.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.assertion: The expansion of access to primary care services in Bangladesh has contributed to improvements in population health.
items.reason: Bangladesh's progress is attributed to a vibrant NGO community focusing on poor women and their families.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)
items.assertion: China's health system has always focused on primary prevention approaches.
items.reason: The lack of primary care services in China has led to late presentation with illness and insufficient investment in primary prevention.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the reason is true but does not support a focus on primary prevention.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2)

items.subtopic: EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)
items.assertion: China and India are using public resources from their growing economies to fund primary care services.
items.reason: Both countries have implemented measures to strengthen primary health care by increasing funding and expanding access.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, and the reason correctly explains that both China and India are using public resources from their growing economies to fund primary care services.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · EXPERIENCES WITH PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 3)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Increased social participation can help build primary care services.
items.reason: Political pressure and entrepreneurial initiatives have accelerated progress in primary care without major funding increases.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)
items.assertion: Community health workers in Ethiopia have contributed to better health outcomes.
items.reason: They are deployed without any access to physician support or team deployment.
items.explanation: The assertion is true, but the reason is false as community health workers do have access to physician support and teams.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 1)

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.assertion: Global interconnectedness in health is growing.
items.reason: Due to shared microbial threats and the flow of migrant health workers.
items.explanation: Both the assertion and reason are true, and the reason correctly explains the assertion.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION

items.subtopic: OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
items.assertion: The COVID-19 pandemic has decreased global political attention to health inequities.
items.reason: It has highlighted glaring shortfalls in health systems and social conditions.
items.explanation: The assertion is false; the passage states that these issues have commanded significant global political attention.
items.reference: Primary Care and Global Health · OPPORTUNITIES TO BUILD PRIMARY CARE IN LOW- AND MIDDLE-INCOME COUNTRIES (part 2) | CONCLUSION
